source_paper_title: Optimal Budget Aggregation with Star-Shaped Preference Domains
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Brandt_2025, title={Optimal Budget Aggregation with Star-Shaped Preference Domains}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0723}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0723}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Brandt, Felix and Greger, Matthias and Segal-Halevi, Erel and Suksompong, Warut}, year={2025}, month=dec }

We conjecture that continuity is required for the characterization as well.

Some concrete open questions are:
• Does Theorem 3 also hold when weakening group-strategyproofness to strategyproofness? Is continuity required for the characterization of the Nash product rule?
title: Characterize Nash product rule under strategyproofness and core fairness conditions
background_latex:
Let there be a set of agents \(N=[n]\) and alternatives \(M=[m]\). Outcomes are distributions \(q\in\Delta^m:=\{q\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}:\sum_{j\in M} q_j=1\}\).

Each agent \(i\in N\) has a unique ideal distribution (peak) \(p_i\in\Delta^m\) and evaluates an outcome \(q\) using Leontief utility
\[
 u_i(q)=\min_{j\in M_i}\frac{q_j}{p_{i,j}},\qquad M_i:=\{j\in M: p_{i,j}>0\}.
\]
An aggregation mechanism is a function \(f:(\Delta^m)^n\to\Delta^m\) mapping the profile of peaks \(P=(p_1,\dots,p_n)\) to an outcome \(f(P)\).

Continuity of \(f\) means: for every \(P\) and \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that \(\|P-P'\|_1<\delta\) implies \(\|f(P)-f(P')\|_1<\varepsilon\), where \(\|\cdot\|_1\) denotes the \(\ell_1\) norm on the finite-dimensional vector space.

Strategyproofness means that no single agent can benefit by misreporting her peak, holding others fixed, where preferences are induced by \(u_i\) with true peak \(p_i\). Group-strategyproofness is the analogous condition for coalitions.

Core-fair-share (a core-based fairness notion) for an outcome \(q\) at profile \(P\) means there is no coalition \(N'\subseteq N\) and distribution \(q'\in\Delta^m\) such that for every \(q''\in\Delta^m\), every agent \(i\in N'\) strictly prefers \((|N'|/n)q' + (1-|N'|/n)q''\) to \(q\) (preferences again induced by \(u_i\)). A mechanism satisfies core fair share if \(f(P)\) has no blocking coalition for every \(P\).

The Nash product rule (Nash welfare maximizer) for Leontief utilities is
\[
\mathrm{NASH}(P)\in\arg\max_{q\in\Delta^m}\prod_{i\in N} u_i(q),
\]
which is known (in the paper) to be single-valued for these utilities.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the following implication holds for Leontief utilities on \(\Delta^m\):

If a mechanism \(f:(\Delta^m)^n\to\Delta^m\) is continuous, satisfies strategyproofness (for the Leontief-induced preferences), and satisfies core fair share, must it coincide with the Nash product rule \(\mathrm{NASH}\) on all profiles \(P\in(\Delta^m)^n\), i.e., must \(f(P)=\mathrm{NASH}(P)\) for every \(P\)?

Equivalently, is group-strategyproofness dispensable in the characterization stating that \(\mathrm{NASH}\) is the unique continuous mechanism satisfying group-strategyproofness and core fair share?
latex:
Let there be a set of agents \(N=[n]\) and alternatives \(M=[m]\). Outcomes are distributions \(q\in\Delta^m:=\{q\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}:\sum_{j\in M} q_j=1\}\).

open_problem_quote:
We conjecture that continuity is required for the characterization as well.

Some concrete open questions are:
• Does Theorem 3 also hold when weakening group-strategyproofness to strategyproofness? Is continuity required for the characterization of the Nash product rule?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample would be an explicit continuous, strategyproof, core-fair-share mechanism distinct from Nash, or a profile witnessing necessity of group-strategyproofness; verifying the axioms is nontrivial but mechanizable for small \(n,m\) by discretizing peaks and checking incentive constraints approximately. Exact verification over the continuous simplex is harder, so brute-force search is only a proxy.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate mechanisms can be parameterized (e.g., perturbations of \(\mathrm{NASH}\), piecewise-defined rules, or convex combinations followed by projection), and a fitness signal can be computed by sampling many profiles and testing approximate strategyproofness/core-blocking constraints plus distance from \(\mathrm{NASH}\). However, enforcing global continuity and exact (not sampled) incentive/core conditions is difficult, so the loop would mainly help discover plausible counterexamples or conjecture refinements rather than certify them.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Brandt–Greger–Segal-Halevi–Suksompong, 2025) introduces Leontief (ratio-based) utilities on the simplex \(\Delta^m\) and shows that, unlike \(\ell_1\) or \(\ell_\infty\) distance-based models, there is a uniquely attractive aggregation rule: the Nash product rule \(\mathrm{NASH}(P)\in\arg\max_{q\in\Delta^m}\prod_i u_i(q)\). They prove \(\mathrm{NASH}\) is continuous, satisfies core fair share (a weak \(\alpha\)-core style blocking condition), and—crucially—obeys group-strategyproofness. Their main characterization (Theorem 3) states that \(\mathrm{NASH}\) is the only continuous mechanism satisfying group-strategyproofness and core fair share.

The open problem asks whether group-strategyproofness can be weakened to (individual) strategyproofness while retaining the same uniqueness conclusion. The source paper itself indicates that group-strategyproofness is used at a specific point in the proof (their Lemma 9), and it proves that for the refined Leximin-Leontief domain, strategyproofness does suffice (Theorem 4). This suggests that the obstacle is tied to indifferences inherent in plain Leontief utilities: coalitional deviations may be needed to pin down the outcome when individual deviations do not move utilities strictly.

Related literature provides partial structural support but no resolution. Brandt et al. (2025) (donor coordination) supplies the key decomposition/critical-alternative machinery and the uniqueness/single-valuedness of Nash-welfare maximizers for Leontief utilities that underlies the characterization, but it does not address whether individual strategyproofness plus core fair share forces the same decomposition invariants. Work on core notions in participatory budgeting and fair mixing (Fain et al., 2016; Aziz et al., 2020) motivates core-based fairness and its connection to Nash welfare, while older economic design results for Leontief preferences (Nicolò, 2004) illustrate the special incentive structure of Leontief domains in low dimensions. Techniques from one-dimensional single-peaked characterizations (Jennings et al., 2024) may be suggestive, but the multi-dimensional simplex setting with Leontief indifferences appears to require new arguments or a counterexample. At present, absent forward-citation evidence of a solution, the implication ‘continuous + strategyproof + core fair share \(\Rightarrow\) \(f\equiv\mathrm{NASH}\)’ should be regarded as open.

sources.title: Coordinating charitable donations with Leontief preferences
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and analyzes the equilibrium distribution rule (EDR), which for Leontief utilities coincides with maximizing Nash welfare; proves uniqueness of the Nash-welfare-maximizing allocation and establishes strong incentive properties (including group-strategyproofness in their model) and core-like stability/no-profitable-redistribution guarantees.
sources.connection: Provides the main technical foundation used by the source paper to prove that the Nash product rule is continuous, single-valued, group-strategyproof, and satisfies a core-type fairness property. While it does not address whether group-strategyproofness can be weakened to strategyproofness in the budget-aggregation characterization, it supplies the equilibrium/decomposition lemmas (critical alternatives, decompositions) that any attempt to remove group-strategyproofness must grapple with.

sources.title: Fair mixing: the case of dichotomous preferences
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies core-type fairness for mixing/lotteries under dichotomous preferences; shows existence and properties of Nash-welfare-type outcomes and links to core notions (including core fair share variants) in probabilistic/fair-mixing settings.
sources.connection: The source paper’s core-fair-share definition is adapted from the fair-mixing/participatory-budgeting literature; this paper provides background on why Nash-welfare maximization is naturally connected to core-type fairness. It does not address strategyproofness vs group-strategyproofness for Leontief peaks, but informs the fairness side of the characterization question.

sources.title: The core of the participatory budgeting problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and studies core concepts for participatory budgeting/public decision problems; establishes existence/structure results for core outcomes and motivates core-based fairness as protection against coalitional deviations.
sources.connection: Provides foundational motivation for core-based fairness (core fair share) used in the source paper’s characterization. It does not treat incentive compatibility for peak-based Leontief utilities, but frames why core-type constraints are central in budget aggregation and why coalitional blocking is the relevant fairness benchmark.

sources.title: Efficiency and truthfulness with Leontief preferences. A note on two-agent, two-good economies
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes incentive and efficiency properties in small economies with Leontief preferences; highlights special structure of Leontief utilities for truthful/efficient allocation rules in low-dimensional settings.
sources.connection: Gives early economic-design context for why Leontief preferences can admit stronger incentive properties than smooth/strictly convex domains. While not about budget aggregation on a simplex nor core fair share, it supports the plausibility that Nash-type rules have distinctive incentive features under Leontief structure.

sources.title: New characterizations of strategy-proofness under single-peakedness
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides new characterizations of strategyproofness in single-peaked domains, refining classical results and clarifying when continuity/proportionality pin down specific median/phantom rules.
sources.connection: Although focused on the one-dimensional (two-alternative) case, it is conceptually relevant because the source paper draws analogies between generalized median rules and Nash-type rules; techniques about weakening group-strategyproofness to strategyproofness in refined domains may inspire approaches for the multi-dimensional Leontief setting, but it does not directly resolve the open problem.
